Make the guajillo chile sauce. Heat water in a kettle or pan. Snip tops off chiles and remove seeds. Toast guajillo chiles over hot pan for 10-15 seconds, turning constantly. Move to bowl and pour hot water over them. Soak 10 minutes until soft. Add chiles to blender with garlic and chicken broth. Blend until smooth. Strain through metal strainer to remove seeds and skin. Taste and add salt if needed. Transfer to airtight container; stores in fridge for weeks. 2. Sauté the chorizo & potato. Heat cast-iron pan over medium heat. Add chorizo to render fat, about 2-3 minutes. Dice potato and add to chorizo. Sauté together 8 minutes until potatoes brown and soften. Note: parboil potatoes after dicing for faster cooking. 3. Soak & sear the bread. Spoon guajillo sauce onto plate and dip bread roll to cover both halves. Add oil drizzle to pan and heat over high heat. Add dipped bread halves and sear both sides. Flatten slightly with spatula. Once toasted, turn off heat. 4. Assemble the sandwich. Add chorizo filling to one bread half. Top with cotija cheese, salsa, shredded lettuce, and pickled onions. Pour in cornstarch gel and blend to create stable emulsion. Taste and adjust salt. Store in fridge up to 3-4 days. 3. Boil pot of salted water, add pasta, cook until slightly underdone (1-2 minutes less than package), reserve pasta water. 4. Cut guanciale into cubes, mince garlic. Add oil to pan, place guanciale cubes, heat medium-low until fat renders and guanciale is golden brown. Add garlic and cayenne, cook 1-2 minutes, turn off heat. Reserve spoonfuls for topping. Add 2-3 spoonfuls carbonara sauce to guanciale pan, mix until smooth, keep warm. 5. In new pan, add drained pasta and spoonfuls of aromatic-infused sauce. Evaluate this meal suggestion for {{party_size}} people with ingredients {{input}}.
Score 1-5 on three criteria, then give an overall score (the minimum of the three):
(1) Practicality: are the dishes executable with the stated ingredients without requiring unusual tools or techniques?
(2) Count fit: is the total number of recipes between {{expected_dish_min}} and {{expected_dish_max}}?
(3) Variety: are the recipes meaningfully different (different protein treatment, cuisine, or technique — not two pan-fried chicken dishes)?
Return the overall score.

The user provided these ingredients: {{input}}. Common pantry staples (salt, pepper, oil, garlic, onion, basic spices, soy sauce, vinegar) are acceptable.
Score 1-5:
5 = All ingredients in recipes are either user-provided, pantry staples, or minor acceptable extras (1-2 per recipe).
3 = Several unlisted non-staple ingredients appear but don't dominate.
1 = Recipes are loaded with ingredients the user didn't mention (e.g., needing butter, cream, specialty produce, fresh herbs not named).
Return the score.

(1) Practicality: are the dishes executable with the stated ingredients without requiring unusual tools or techniques?
(2) Count fit: is the total number of recipes between {{expected_dish_min}} and {{expected_dish_max}}?
(3) Variety: are the recipes meaningfully different (different protein treatment, cuisine, or technique — not two pan-fried chicken dishes)?
Return the overall score.

The user provided these ingredients: {{input}}. Common pantry staples (salt, pepper, oil, garlic, onion, basic spices, soy sauce, vinegar) are acceptable.
Score 1-5:
5 = All ingredients in recipes are either user-provided, pantry staples, or minor acceptable extras (1-2 per recipe).
3 = Several unlisted non-staple ingredients appear but don't dominate.
1 = Recipes are loaded with ingredients the user didn't mention (e.g., needing butter, cream, specialty produce, fresh herbs not named).
Return the score.

assert.value:
Evaluate this vegetarian (no-dairy) meal suggestion for {{party_size}} people with ingredients {{input}}.
Score 1-5 on three criteria, then give an overall score (the minimum of the three):
(1) Practicality: are the dishes executable with the stated vegetarian ingredients (tofu, mushrooms) without unusual tools or techniques?
(2) Count fit: is the total number of recipes between {{expected_dish_min}} and {{expected_dish_max}}?
(3) Variety: are the recipes meaningfully different (different technique or cuisine — not two near-identical stir-fries)?
Return the overall score.

The user said vegetarian AND no dairy. Score 1-5:
5 = Zero violations (no meat/poultry/fish/seafood/gelatin; no milk/cream/butter/cheese/yogurt). At least one concrete recipe is present.
3 = Mostly compliant but a borderline ingredient is ambiguous (e.g., "broth" without specifying vegetable broth).
1 = Clear violation — any dairy or animal protein appears.
Return the score.

3. Set a wok or large pan over high heat and add a drizzle of neutral oil. Quickly saute the garlic and shallots for 45 seconds or until fragrant. Add the onions and peppers and stir fry until slightly softened and browned. Remove the vegetable mixture from the pan and set aside in a bowl.
4. Add another drizzle of oil to the wok. Once hot, add in the marinated beef slices and let those sizzle and brown on one side without moving. After 2 minutes, start moving it around, trying to get browning on all sides. Once the beef is browned and almost cooked through (but not overcooked), add the vegetables back in.
5. Add about half of the black pepper sauce and stir it all together to spread the flavors around. If you want the dish to be saucier, you can add more at this point. 3 = Mostly compliant but a borderline ingredient is ambiguous (e.g., "broth" without specifying vegetable broth).
1 = Clear violation — any dairy or animal protein appears.
Return the score.

tests.description: C3: vague input — no ingredients, no party size

vars.input: I want to cook something

assert.value:
The user gave a vague prompt. Score 1-5 on three criteria, return the minimum:
(1) Acknowledgment: does the response recognize the input is underspecified?
(2) Forward motion: does it EITHER ask 1-2 targeted clarifying questions OR make a small reasonable assumption and proceed (not both, not neither)?
(3) Usefulness: if assumptions were made, is the output still usable? If clarifications were asked, are they the right ones (ingredients on hand, party size, dietary constraints)?
Return the overall score.

vars.input: I have chicken breast, rice, and broccoli, and I need halal recipes. Please help me plan dinner for two people.

assert.value:

Score 1-5 where:
5 = Zero violations AND at least one concrete recipe is present.
3 = Mostly compliant with minor concerns (e.g., a borderline ingredient like unspecified broth).
1 = Clear violation (e.g., pork, alcohol, non-halal gelatin appears), or no concrete recipe present.
Return the score.

tests.description: D3: recipes swap with 2-turn history — vegetarian swap

assert.value:
Context: user asked for 3 dinners with chicken/rice/vegetables for 4, then asked to swap the first recipe for something vegetarian. Judge the FINAL response only.
Score 1-5 on three criteria, return the minimum:

(2) Count preserved: total recipes = 3 (not 4, not 2).
(3) Continuity: recipes 2 and 3 are the same or consistent with the previous turn (the user only asked to change recipe 1).
